1.synthesis: One of the seven bowl-angels invites John, 'Come, I will show you the judgment of the great prostitute who is seated on many waters' (v.1). In the Spirit he is carried to a wilderness and sees a woman seated on a scarlet beast full of blasphemous names, with seven heads and ten horns (v.3). She is arrayed in purple and scarlet, glittering with gold and jewels, holding 'a golden cup full of abominations and the impurities of her sexual immorality' (v.4); on her forehead is written, 'Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes and of earth's abominations' (v.5). She is 'drunk with the blood of the saints' (v.6). The integrative thread is the unmasking of seductive worldly power: what looks gloriously rich is, at its heart, idolatry and bloodshed — the great city revealed as a harlot.
1.historical_context: The figure draws on the prophets' portrayal of Tyre and Nineveh as prostitutes who seduce the nations with commerce and idolatry (Isaiah 23:17; Nahum 3:4), and especially on the oracles against Babylon (Jeremiah 51:13, the city 'who dwells by many waters'). 'Many waters' is decoded within the chapter as 'peoples and multitudes and nations' (v.15). The purple and scarlet, gold and jewels, and the golden cup picture Rome's opulence and the intoxicating allure of her trade and cult. Commentators in the Reformation tradition (Gill, Henry, Wesley) extended the harlot beyond pagan Rome to apostate, persecuting religion — Gill notes that harlots wrote their names on their foreheads, an image of brazen, public idolatry. The first and most natural referent for John's readers, however, is imperial Rome, drunk on the blood of the martyrs.
1.literary_structure: The harlot is the deliberate antithesis of two other women in Revelation: the sun-clothed woman of Revelation 12 who bears the Messiah, and the bride of the Lamb, the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2). The book sets two cities against each other — Babylon the harlot and Jerusalem the bride — and forces a choice. The 'golden cup full of abominations' (v.4) ironically inverts the golden bowls of wrath: she who made the nations drink her seductions will be made to drink judgment. That John is carried 'into a wilderness' (v.3) to see her, while the bride is shown from a great mountain (Revelation 21:10), marks the contrast in vantage as well as object.
1.theological_application: The vision teaches discernment. Worldly power presents itself as beauty, wealth, and security; Revelation strips away the gilding to show idolatry and the blood of the innocent underneath. The church's danger is not only persecution but seduction — being intoxicated by Babylon's cup. The later call, 'Come out of her, my people' (Revelation 18:4), is the practical application already implied here: refuse the harlot's allurements. John's 'great amazement' at the woman (v.6) is itself a warning — even the seer is briefly dazzled, and the angel must correct him. Believers must learn to see the world-system as God sees it, glory and gore together.
1.christology: The harlot is 'drunk with the blood of the saints and the blood of the martyrs of Jesus' (v.6) — her enmity is ultimately against Christ in his members, as the persecutor on the Damascus road heard, 'why are you persecuting me?' (Acts 9:4). She is the counterfeit bride; the true Bridegroom has a faithful bride for whom he gave himself (Ephesians 5:25–27). The blood she sheds is answered by the blood of the Lamb that purchased those very saints (Revelation 5:9); the martyrs conquer 'by the blood of the Lamb' (Revelation 12:11). Babylon's cup of abomination stands over against the cup of the new covenant in Christ's blood (Luke 22:20).

7.synthesis: The angel explains 'the mystery of the woman, and of the beast with seven heads and ten horns that carries her' (v.7). The beast 'was, and is not, and is about to rise from the bottomless pit and go to destruction' (v.8). The seven heads are 'seven mountains on which the woman is seated,' and also 'seven kings' (vv.9–10); the ten horns are ten kings who receive authority for one hour with the beast (v.12). 'They will make war on the Lamb, and the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings' (v.14). Then comes the turn: the ten horns and the beast 'will hate the prostitute... and burn her up with fire' (v.16), for God has put it into their hearts to fulfill his purpose (v.17). The thread is sovereign irony: evil divides and devours itself, accomplishing God's word.
7.historical_context: 'Seven mountains' (v.9) is an almost transparent pointer to Rome, the city famed for its seven hills — first-century readers could not miss it. The 'seven kings,' of whom 'five have fallen, one is, the other has not yet come' (v.10), have been variously identified with successive Roman emperors, with successive world-empires, or with kingdoms across history; Gill surveys the options and the chapter itself flags the difficulty — 'here is the mind that has wisdom' (v.9). The beast that 'was, and is not, and is to come' parodies the eternal God 'who is and who was and who is to come' (Revelation 1:8) and likely plays on the Nero redivivus legend that the dead persecutor would return. The ten horns recall the ten-horned fourth beast of Daniel 7:7, 24.
7.literary_structure: The section is an angelic interpretation, a form familiar from Daniel 7:16 and Zechariah 4, where the seer asks and a heavenly figure decodes the symbols. Its rhetorical center is the abrupt reversal at v.16: the beast and its horns destroy the harlot they carried. This self-consuming character of evil is a theme of the prophets (Ezekiel 23:22–25, where Oholibah's lovers turn on her). The double decoding of the heads (mountains and kings) is intentional layering — the same symbol carries geographic and dynastic meaning, resisting any single flat reading and signaling that 'wisdom' is required. The Lamb's victory (v.14) is stated in advance, before the battle of Revelation 19, as the interpretive key.
7.theological_application: 'God has put it into their hearts to carry out his purpose' (v.17) is one of Scripture's clearest statements of God's sovereignty over the very actions of his enemies — without excusing their malice, he bends it to fulfill his word (cf. Genesis 50:20; Acts 4:27–28). The fall of Babylon comes not only by the Lamb's sword but through the internal treachery of evil, which cannot finally cohere. The pastoral comfort is immense: the powers arrayed against the church are not a stable, eternal order but a self-destructing coalition under God's hand. Believers are called to be 'with him... called and chosen and faithful' (v.14) — perseverance, not panic.
7.christology: At the heart of the explanation is a doxological declaration: 'the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings' (v.14) — a title given in the Old Testament to the LORD himself (Deuteronomy 10:17; Psalm 136:3) and now borne by the slain Lamb, repeated at his triumphant appearing (Revelation 19:16). The beast that 'was, and is not, and is to come' is the dark parody of the Christ who 'was dead, and behold I am alive forevermore' (Revelation 1:18); the counterfeit goes 'to destruction,' but the true King reigns. Those with him share his victory because they are 'called and chosen and faithful' — election, calling, and persevering faith, all secured in the Lamb.
